content.text: Now let me implement both features by modifying the groupby filter functions.

input.old_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, include_empty: bool = True, key_transform: t.Optional[t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can set ``include_empty`` to ``False`` to exclude items whose grouping attribute is ``None``, an empty string, or otherwise falsy. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", include_empty=False) %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can pass a ``key_transform`` callable to format the group key used for display, without affecting how items are grouped. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for year, items in users|groupby("birthday.year", key_transform=str) %}
  • {{ year }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.2 Added the ``include_empty`` and ``key_transform`` parameters. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if not include_empty: value = [item for item in value if expr(item)] out = [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] if key_transform is not None: out = [_GroupTuple(key_transform(key), values) for key, values in out] return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, include_empty: bool = True, key_transform: t.Optional[t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) values = await auto_to_list(value) if not include_empty: values = [item for item in values if expr(item)] out = [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(subvalues)) for key, subvalues in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr) ] if key_transform is not None: out = [_GroupTuple(key_transform(key), subvalues) for key, subvalues in out] return out

The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr)

""" expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if not include_empty: value = [item for item in value if expr(item)] out = [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] if key_transform is not None: out = [_GroupTuple(key_transform(key), values) for key, values in out] return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, include_empty: bool = True, key_transform: t.Optional[t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) values = await auto_to_list(value) if not include_empty: values = [item for item in values if expr(item)] out = [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(subvalues)) for key, subvalues in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr) ] if key_transform is not None: out = [_GroupTuple(key_transform(key), subvalues) for key, subvalues in out] return out

tool_use_result.oldString:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]

The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Set ``include_empty`` to ``False`` to exclude items whose grouping attribute is ``None``, an empty string, or otherwise falsy. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", include_empty=False) %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}

    {% for year, items in users|groupby("birthday.year", key_transform=str) %}

content.text: Now let's verify it works with quick tests.

input.command:
python3 -c "
from jinja2 import Environment

env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\", include_empty=False) %}{{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\", \") }}\n{% endfor %}')
users = [
    {'name': 'Alice', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'Bob', 'city': None},
    {'name': 'Carl', 'city': ''},
    {'name': 'Dan', 'city': 'NY'},
]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))
print('---')
tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\", key_transform=lambda x: x.upper() if x else x) %}{{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\", \") }}\n{% endfor %}')
print(tmpl2.render(users=[{'name':'Alice','city':'ny'},{'name':'Bob','city':'ny'}]))
"

content.text: That's expected — Jinja templates don't support lambda syntax; the first test (include_empty) passed correctly. Let's test key_transform passing a Python callable via globals.

input.command:
python3 -c "
from jinja2 import Environment

env = Environment()
env.globals['upper_or_none'] = lambda x: x.upper() if x else x
tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\", key_transform=upper_or_none) %}{{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\", \") }}\n{% endfor %}')
print(tmpl2.render(users=[{'name':'Alice','city':'ny'},{'name':'Bob','city':'ny'}]))
"

content.text: Both features work. Let's also run the existing test suite for groupby to ensure nothing broke.
All existing groupby tests pass. Now let's generate the patch file.

content.text: That's actually correctly formatted (two blank lines between top-level definitions is PEP8 standard). Let's run black/flake8 if available to double check style consistency.
Not available, but the formatting matches the file's existing style. Let's run the broader test suite to confirm nothing else broke.

All 837 tests pass. The patch is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`.

Summary: Added `include_empty` (default `True`) and `key_transform` (default `None`) parameters to both `sync_do_groupby` and the async `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`. `include_empty=False` filters out items whose grouping attribute is falsy before grouping; `key_transform` applies a callable to each group's key after grouping without affecting grouping logic itself. Both remain backward-compatible defaults, docstrings updated, and full test suite (837 tests) passes.

result:
All 837 tests pass. The patch is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`.

Summary: Added `include_empty` (default `True`) and `key_transform` (default `None`) parameters to both `sync_do_groupby` and the async `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`. `include_empty=False` filters out items whose grouping attribute is falsy before grouping; `key_transform` applies a callable to each group's key after grouping without affecting grouping logic itself. Both remain backward-compatible defaults, docstrings updated, and full test suite (837 tests) passes.
